text: B.F. Skinner's most unsettling discovery was not that rewards shape behavior — everyone knew that. It was that intermittent rewards shape behavior far more powerfully than consistent ones. In his variable-ratio reinforcement schedule, a lever delivered food pellets on an unpredictable basis. Rats pressed the lever obsessively, far more than when every press produced a pellet. The unpredictability itself was the engine. Skinner had stumbled onto a principle that would later underpin gambling machines, loot boxes, and infinite feeds: when you cannot predict whether this action will pay off, you cannot easily stop performing it. Certainty extinguishes compulsion; uncertainty inflames it.
question: Why does variable-ratio reinforcement produce more persistent behavior than consistent reward schedules?

options.text: Because the rewards are larger when they do arrive
Because the unpredictability of the reward prevents the behavior from extinguishing
Because the brain stores variable rewards in a separate memory system
Because animals learn to anticipate the pattern and time their actions accordingly
